```json
[
  "\"Foundations of Computer Vision\" is a 2024 MIT Press book (ISBN: 9780262378666) by Antonio Torralba, Phillip Isola, and William Freeman for undergraduate/graduate students and practitioners, conceptualized in November 2010, taking over 10 years to complete, and influenced by the 2012 deep learning revolution.",
  "The book is organized into 15 parts covering foundational topics: Parts I-III address motivational introduction, mathematical tools, and image formation; Parts IV-V cover learning foundations, signal processing, and linear filters; Parts VI-X address multiscale representations, neural networks for vision (CNNs, RNNs, transformers), statistical and graphical models, generative modeling and representation learning, and challenges of learning-based vision systems; Parts XI-XIII cover geometry for 3D reconstruction, motion processing and sequences, and scene understanding with object detection; Part XIV provides advice for junior researchers on presentations, writing papers, and researcher mentality; and Part XV applies techniques to solve the toy problem introduced in Part I.",
  "Each chapter is limited to five pages or less, and the book focuses on foundational concepts rather than current state-of-the-art methods or applications such as shape analysis, object tracking, person pose analysis, or face recognition."
]
```
json_data: "Foundations of Computer Vision" is a 2024 MIT Press book (ISBN: 9780262378666) by Antonio Torralba, Phillip Isola, and William Freeman for undergraduate/graduate students and practitioners, conceptualized in November 2010, taking over 10 years to complete, and influenced by the 2012 deep learning revolution.

The book is organized into 15 parts covering foundational topics: Parts I-III address motivational introduction, mathematical tools, and image formation; Parts IV-V cover learning foundations, signal processing, and linear filters; Parts VI-X address multiscale representations, neural networks for vision (CNNs, RNNs, transformers), statistical and graphical models, generative modeling and representation learning, and challenges of learning-based vision systems; Parts XI-XIII cover geometry for 3D reconstruction, motion processing and sequences, and scene understanding with object detection; Part XIV provides advice for junior researchers on presentations, writing papers, and researcher mentality; and Part XV applies techniques to solve the toy problem introduced in Part I.

Each chapter is limited to five pages or less, and the book focuses on foundational concepts rather than current state-of-the-art methods or applications such as shape analysis, object tracking, person pose analysis, or face recognition.
